---
title: "Sequence Alignment — Notes"
subtitle: "Lecture 01"
---

These notes are the reference version of the session. The slides are
deliberately sparser; everything stated in passing during the lecture is
written out here.

## Why align sequences?

Sequence comparison underlies most of bioinformatics: annotation transfer,
phylogeny, variant calling, database search. The reasoning runs from an
observation to an inference:

> Two sequences are **similar** (measurable). They may therefore be
> **homologous**, i.e. descended from a common ancestor (inferred).

The distinction matters. Similarity is a property of the strings in front of
you. Homology is a historical claim, and a claim that similarity supports but
never proves — convergent evolution and chance both produce similarity without
shared ancestry. Students routinely collapse the two terms, and it is worth
being pedantic about it early.

## Scoring an alignment

{{< include /shared/partials/scoring-scheme.qmd >}}

The scheme in @tbl-scoring is deliberately crude. Real work uses substitution
matrices (PAM, BLOSUM) whose entries are log-odds ratios derived from observed
substitution frequencies in curated alignments, so that chemically conservative
substitutions cost less than radical ones.

### Gap penalties

A single constant gap penalty treats a run of ten gaps as ten times as costly
as one. Biologically that is wrong: a single indel event of length ten is far
more likely than ten independent events. Hence *affine* gap costs, which charge
more to open a gap than to extend it:

$$
\gamma(n) = -d - (n-1) \cdot e \qquad \text{with } d > e
$$

## Needleman–Wunsch

The recurrence given on the slides is a dynamic programming formulation: the
optimal alignment of two prefixes is built from optimal alignments of shorter
prefixes. Two things are worth stressing:

1. **Initialisation encodes the algorithm's intent.** For global alignment the
   first row and column are filled with cumulative gap penalties, because a
   prefix aligned against nothing *is* a run of gaps. Smith–Waterman
   initialises to zero instead — that single change is what makes it local.
2. **The matrix gives the score; the traceback gives the alignment.** They are
   separate steps, and the traceback may be ambiguous when several predecessors
   tie. Multiple optimal alignments are normal, not a bug.

## Further reading

- Durbin et al., *Biological Sequence Analysis*, ch. 2
- Needleman & Wunsch (1970), *J Mol Biol* 48(3):443–453
